## Robustness Assessment [PLACEHOLDER]
<!-- [PLACEHOLDER] -->

This section shifts from training stochasticity to
sensitivity to modeling choices. The question is whether the
conclusions for the current high-frequency TD3 setup would
change materially under nearby alternatives in feature specification, reward
definition, transaction-cost assumptions, or split design.

The goal is to separate results that appear structural from results that may
depend too heavily on one narrow experimental setup.

```{python}
#| label: robustness_setup
#| echo: false
#| warning: false

import pandas as pd

from thesis_mlflow_results import load_experiment_snapshot, runs_overview_table

EXPERIMENT_NAME = "TRADINGENV_TD3_AAPL_HFT_STATE_SPACE"
snapshot = load_experiment_snapshot(EXPERIMENT_NAME)
finished = snapshot.latest_finished
runs_df = runs_overview_table(EXPERIMENT_NAME)
```

### Why Robustness Matters in This Thesis [PLACEHOLDER]
<!-- [PLACEHOLDER] -->

In this work, robustness matters because the agent is trained on a noisy
microstructure-driven reward stream and evaluated in a simplified execution
environment. Apparent improvements may therefore come from favorable training
dynamics or feature-set idiosyncrasies rather than from a genuinely more useful
policy. A result is treated as robust only if it remains directionally
consistent under reasonable changes in seed, state representation, reward
specification, and cost assumptions, while preserving interpretable turnover and
drawdown behavior.

### Current Evaluation Context [PLACEHOLDER]
<!-- [PLACEHOLDER] -->

The following snapshot provides the current experiment context used in the
automated Chapter 6 result wiring. It is included to make the robustness
discussion explicit about which experimental setup the displayed results refer to.

```{python}
#| echo: false

if finished is None:
    print(f"No finished runs available for experiment: {EXPERIMENT_NAME}")
else:
    params = finished.get("params", {})
    latest = finished.get("latest_metrics")
    rows = [
        ("Experiment", EXPERIMENT_NAME),
        ("Run Name", finished.get("run_name")),
        ("Seed", params.get("seed")),
        ("Algorithm", params.get("training_algorithm")),
        ("Reward Type", params.get("env_reward_type")),
        ("Train Size", params.get("data_train_size")),
        ("Feature Set", "Causal LOB microstructure feature set"),
        ("Finished Runs in Snapshot", 0 if runs_df.empty else int((runs_df["status"] == "FINISHED").sum())),
        ("Latest Training Steps", None if latest is None else latest.get("training_steps")),
    ]
    display(pd.DataFrame(rows, columns=["Field", "Value"]))
```

### Robustness Dimensions Considered in This Thesis [PLACEHOLDER]
<!-- [PLACEHOLDER] -->

The robustness framework is centered on five dimensions most
directly connected to the current empirical design:

1. **Random-seed sensitivity**: whether performance is stable across repeated
   runs of the same setup.
2. **Feature-set sensitivity**: if the richer state-space design improves
   results relative to simpler snapshot-heavy alternatives without introducing
   unstable behavior.
3. **Reward-function sensitivity**: whether conclusions depend on the specific
   training objective used by the agent.
4. **Transaction-cost sensitivity**: whether apparent gains survive realistic
   fee assumptions rather than being generated by excessive turnover.
5. **Data-split sensitivity**: whether results remain similar under reasonable
   changes in the chronological split or training-window length.

These dimensions are sufficient to test if the conclusions here are
specific to one narrow setup or remain credible under nearby variations.

### Robustness Matrix for Experimental Reporting [PLACEHOLDER]
<!-- [PLACEHOLDER] -->

The table below defines a practical robustness matrix for the evaluation part of
the thesis. It can be used as a reporting checklist when final results are
compiled.

```{python}
#| echo: false

robustness_rows = [
    ("Random seed", "seed", "Stochastic training path sensitivity", ">= 5 seeds per selected setup", "Required"),
    ("Feature subset", "env.feature_columns / feature pipeline", "State representation redundancy and overfitting", "All-features vs reduced non-redundant set", "Required"),
    ("Reward function", "reward_type", "Objective-function dependence", "log_return vs differential_sharpe", "Recommended"),
    ("Transaction costs", "trading_fees", "Turnover-driven performance inflation", "Zero-cost and nonzero-cost comparison", "Required"),
    ("Train split length", "train_size / split design", "Sample size and regime exposure sensitivity", "Short vs longer train windows", "Recommended"),
]

robustness_df = pd.DataFrame(
    robustness_rows,
    columns=["Dimension", "Control Variable", "Primary Risk Addressed", "Assessment Design", "Priority"],
)
display(robustness_df)
```

### Interpreting Noisy Training Curves in Robustness Terms [PLACEHOLDER]
<!-- [PLACEHOLDER] -->

Noisy training curves are common in actor-critic methods and should not be
treated as the primary robustness criterion. In the present workflow, the more
informative question is whether the same setup produces similar
evaluation outcomes across repeated runs and whether qualitative behavior, such
as persistent overtrading or directional bias, changes systematically when the
feature set, reward, or cost assumptions are varied.

This matters in the current work because one of the central comparisons
concerns simpler versus broader order-book-derived state representations. If the richer feature set improves returns but materially
increases instability or turnover, the result is weaker than a modest but more
stable improvement.

### Methodological Limits of the Current Robustness Snapshot [PLACEHOLDER]
<!-- [PLACEHOLDER] -->

The current snapshot-based Chapter 6 integration supports reproducible loading
of run summaries and evaluation artifacts, but robustness conclusions remain
conditional on the set of experiments already executed and exported. A single
finished run can illustrate workflow behavior, but it cannot support strong
claims about robustness across seeds, feature sets, or cost regimes.

For this reason, robustness findings in the final thesis should be framed only
to the extent supported by repeated runs and matched comparison settings. The
framework in this section provides the structure for that interpretation and
helps prevent post-hoc emphasis on isolated favorable outcomes.
